Okay, I want to talk about giving promotions. So a common question is what qualifies someone for a promotion, right? Okay, some examples. Experience in the job or tenure, right? So somebody's been doing it for a really long time and they're really great at it, right? Two, high performance level for the most recent review cycles, okay? So they're extremely high performer, they're getting the job done, they're doing a great job, right? Three, skill set that matches the minimum requirements of the new role. So looking at what they have, they're doing right now, they're doing a great job of what they're doing right now, but more importantly, they have all the skills and all the characteristics to do the next job, okay? Four, is they have the personal motivation and willingness for the change in their responsibilities. A common one for that would be that you already see that they are able to delegate, you already see that they are a servant leader and they're not even in a leadership position, right? So they kind of already start showing and doing those things for the next role in the role that they're at present day. And lastly is that they would have the ability to train up a new hire to fill their old role. So something that's kind of like a graduation is like, if you want to graduate to the next one, then you have to help train people to fill you, right? So what if someone wants a promotion but they're not qualified, right? So you've got the trainer say, and they want to be the operations manager and you're like, bro, like, but you're not qualified, okay? What do you do? One, give a stress assignment, okay? So what's a stress assignment? It's one that is beyond your role and responsibility and actually encompassing the responsibilities of the role they desire, right? And so it's an opportunity for the teammate to show the supervisor of the true measure of their abilities. So say the trainer wants to be the operations manager and you say, all right, well, they already worked for you. So you have that advantage. So you could give them five to 10 days and you say, you're the acting operations manager for the next five to 10 days. There's a duration given and then you explain exactly what they need to do during that time to be successful at that role, right? And so this is a chance for you to put them under rapid fire and see if they can show up like the person they think they are, right? So it's a really good way to test people. Two is suggest taking initiative on things involved in the desired role. So if somebody says that they would like to be starting, they would like to do that next role. I would be like, okay, cool. So you should start doing it now because the easiest and the best way to get promoted is to just start doing the role that you want to be promoted to before you even have the title because then it kind of defaults. If you're good at it and you're taking initiative and you're starting to do that role, then yeah, we're going to want you to be promoted and do that role, right? Three, you want your to let your team know to ask one thing that they can improve at to get promoted and give specific examples. So a lot of times people ask me like, okay, what is one thing I can do to show you that I'm worthy of this promotion? And then can you give me examples of that, right? And so just picking where's the biggest area that they need to improve to then be a better fit for that role, right? And then lastly, letting your teammate ask you what should I start doing? What should I stop doing? What should I continue doing, right? And so being very transparent with them, like, you know, you should start doing this. You need to stop complaining, talking crap, whatever it may be. You should continue, you know, loving on the members, doing this, doing that. And so just telling them, like, what are the things that they're doing in their present day role, whether it be skills or qualities that they're exhibiting that you want them to start doing more of, stop doing or continue doing, right? So following that, it's like, okay, how do you not get promoted? So this is something that I think is important to communicate to your team, right? How do you not get promoted? I talk about this with an RT. One, you don't follow the basic guidelines of your role and you can continuously execute on already existing responsibilities, right? And so you are not actually even reaching the standards of the role that you're currently in, right? Which would then probably put you on like a performance review and you probably gotten rid of or gotten written up or you're being coached extra, but either way, that's definitely not the time for promotion, right? Two is inability to ask for feedback and unwillingness to hear the supervisor when it's given. If you cannot take feedback, then you can't grow, which means you'll never get promoted. If you're that person that's constantly like, oh, no, like every time they give me feedback, they're so mean, they're mean. It's like, well, then you're not mature enough to be promoted, right? So lack of soft skills to do the job. A lot of promotions are into leadership positions. And so a lot of people that are really good at doing one thing have a really hard time when it comes to dealing with people because a lot of the times people think, I'm a great trainer, I would be a great manager of trainers. But in reality, managing trainers means conflict resolution, decisiveness, leadership skills. You won't be training sessions. You won't be doing macros or meal plans. You won't be doing programming. So it's a completely different job. It just happens to share the same department, right? And lastly, you don't take initiative, meaning if you see problems, you're just documenting them rather than taking action and telling management and owners what you can do to help them, right? And so remember, anyone can drop a complaint into their weekly report or survey, but it takes a leader to step up and say, hey, I see this is happening and this isn't going well, and here's what I'm going to do about it, right? And those are the kind of people that get promoted.
